nodes.name: International Organizations
nodes.description: Major international bodies — founding, headquarters, membership, and purpose

items.prompt: Which international organization, founded in 1945 to replace the League of Nations, is headquartered in New York and has 193 member states?
items.explanation:
Founded October 24, 1945; charter signed by 50 nations in San Francisco on June 26, 1945; Poland signed later as the 51st founding member
Headquartered in New York City on international territory along the East River
Replaced the League of Nations (1920–1946), which failed to prevent World War II
Five permanent Security Council members with veto power (P5): USA, UK, France, Russia, China
Universal Declaration of Human Rights adopted December 10, 1948 — drafting committee chaired by Eleanor Roosevelt, principally drafted by René Cassin
October 24 is observed annually as United Nations Day
Specialized agencies include WHO, UNESCO, UNICEF, and the World Bank

items.prompt: Which 27-member political bloc, formally established by the Maastricht Treaty in 1993, won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2012 and uses the euro as its common currency?
items.explanation:
Formally established November 1, 1993 by the Maastricht Treaty; built on the earlier European Economic Community (EEC, 1957)
Headquartered in Brussels, Belgium; European Parliament sits in Strasbourg, France
27 member states after the UK departed in 2020 (Brexit)
Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2012 for advancing peace, reconciliation, democracy, and human rights in Europe
The euro (€) is the common currency of 21 member states (the Eurozone) after Bulgaria joined on January 1, 2025
Founding members of the EEC: France, West Germany, Italy, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg
Schengen Area allows passport-free travel across 29 European countries

items.prompt: Which military alliance, founded in 1949 and headquartered in Brussels, commits its 32 members to collective defense under Article 5?
items.explanation:
Founded April 4, 1949 by the North Atlantic Treaty (Washington Treaty); 12 original members
Headquartered in Brussels, Belgium (moved from Paris in 1966 after France withdrew from military command)
32 member countries as of 2024 after Sweden joined on March 7, 2024
Article 5: an attack on one member is an attack on all — invoked only once, after 9/11
Created as a counterweight to the Soviet Union during the Cold War; the Soviet bloc formed the Warsaw Pact (1955) in response
France withdrew from NATO’s military command in 1966 under de Gaulle; rejoined in 2009
Name stands for North Atlantic Treaty Organization

items.prompt: Which specialized agency of the United Nations, founded in 1948 and headquartered in Geneva, led the global campaign that eradicated smallpox in 1980?
items.explanation:
Founded April 7, 1948; April 7 is observed annually as World Health Day
Headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland; a specialized agency of the United Nations
194 member states — nearly every country in the world
Led the successful global campaign to eradicate smallpox, certified in 1980 — the only human disease ever fully eradicated
Declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern on January 30, 2020
Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus (2017–present) is the first African to lead the organization
Classifies diseases and publishes the International Classification of Diseases (ICD), used worldwide

items.prompt: Which Geneva-based trade body, established in 1995 as the successor to GATT, oversees global trade rules among its 166 member countries?
items.explanation:
Founded January 1, 1995; replaced the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT, 1947)
Headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland; 166 members (including the EU, Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan)
Oversees trade agreements, settles trade disputes, and monitors national trade policies
The Doha Development Round (launched 2001) stalled over disagreements between developed and developing nations
China joined in 2001 after 15 years of negotiations — a landmark accession
Russia joined in 2012, making it one of the last major economies to become a member
Dispute settlement system often called the “jewel in the crown” of international trade law

items.prompt: Which international financial institution, conceived at the 1944 Bretton Woods conference and headquartered in Washington, D.C., provides emergency loans to countries facing balance-of-payments crises?
items.explanation:
Founded in 1944 at the Bretton Woods Conference in New Hampshire; began operations in 1945
Headquartered in Washington, D.C.; 191 member countries
Provides emergency financial assistance and economic policy advice to countries in crisis
Special Drawing Rights (SDRs) serve as an international reserve asset and unit of account
Voting power is weighted by financial contribution — the U.S. holds the largest share and effective veto power
Often paired with the World Bank as a “Bretton Woods institution” — both conceived at the same 1944 conference
Managing Director is traditionally a European; current head is Kristalina Georgieva (2019–present)

items.prompt: Which Bretton Woods institution, headquartered in Washington, D.C., provides loans and grants to developing countries for capital projects aimed at reducing poverty?
alternates: International Bank for Reconstruction and Development
items.explanation:
Founded in 1944 at the Bretton Woods Conference alongside the IMF; began operations in 1946
Headquartered in Washington, D.C.; 189 member countries
Originally the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD), created to rebuild post-WWII Europe
Now focused on poverty reduction and development in low- and middle-income countries
The World Bank Group includes five institutions: IBRD, IDA, IFC, MIGA, and ICSID
President is traditionally an American (counterpart to the IMF’s European managing director)
Funds projects in infrastructure, education, health, and governance worldwide

items.prompt: Which Vienna-based cartel of oil-producing nations, founded in Baghdad in 1960, coordinates petroleum production quotas to influence global oil prices?
alternates: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
items.explanation:
Founded September 14, 1960 in Baghdad by five founding members: Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and Venezuela
Headquartered in Vienna, Austria since 1965 (originally Geneva)
12 member countries as of 2025; Saudi Arabia is the dominant producer
Coordinates petroleum production quotas among members to stabilize oil markets and prices
The 1973 oil embargo (Arab members) quadrupled oil prices and caused a global energy crisis
OPEC+ (formed 2016) includes non-member allies like Russia for broader production coordination
Controls roughly 30–40% of global oil supply and about 80% of proven reserves

items.prompt: Which continental organization of 55 African member states, headquartered in Addis Ababa, was established in 2002 to replace the Organisation of African Unity?
items.explanation:
Founded July 9, 2002 in Durban, South Africa; replaced the Organisation of African Unity (OAU, 1963–2002)
Headquartered in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia; 55 member states — all African countries
Modeled partly on the European Union, with a commission, parliament, and court
The AU headquarters building in Addis Ababa was funded and built by China (opened 2012) — a symbol of Chinese investment in Africa
Motto: “A United and Strong Africa”
Suspends members after unconstitutional changes of government (coups)
Morocco rejoined in 2017 after a 33-year absence over the Western Sahara dispute

items.prompt: Which regional bloc of Southeast Asian nations, founded in Bangkok in 1967 and headquartered in Jakarta, now has 11 members after its newest joined in 2025?
alternates: The Association of Southeast Asian Nations
items.explanation:
Founded August 8, 1967 by the Bangkok Declaration; five founding members: Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand
Headquartered in Jakarta, Indonesia; 11 member states after Timor-Leste joined on October 26, 2025
Members: Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Timor-Leste, Vietnam
Combined GDP of roughly $3.6 trillion — would be the 5th-largest economy if counted as one country
Operates by consensus; the “ASEAN Way” emphasizes non-interference in members’ internal affairs
ASEAN Regional Forum (ARF) includes 27 countries for security dialogue, including the U.S., China, and Russia
Timor-Leste was approved for membership in 2022 and formally acceded in 2025

items.prompt: Which humanitarian organization, inspired by the 1859 Battle of Solferino, was co-founded by Henry Dunant in 1863 and has won the Nobel Peace Prize three times?
alternates: International Committee of the Red Cross
items.explanation:
Founded February 17, 1863 in Geneva by Henry Dunant and four others after Dunant witnessed the Battle of Solferino (1859)
Headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland
Won the Nobel Peace Prize three times: 1917, 1944 (during both World Wars), and 1963 (centennial year)
Henry Dunant shared the first-ever Nobel Peace Prize in 1901 with Frédéric Passy
The red cross emblem is the Swiss flag with reversed colors — a tribute to Switzerland’s neutrality
The Red Crescent is used in Muslim-majority countries; the Red Crystal is a religiously neutral alternative
Inspired the Geneva Conventions (1864, revised 1949), the foundation of international humanitarian law

items.prompt: Which Lyon-based international policing body, with 196 member countries, facilitates cross-border law enforcement cooperation through its system of color-coded notices?
items.explanation:
Founded 1923 in Vienna as the International Criminal Police Commission; reformed in 1956 as INTERPOL
Headquartered in Lyon, France since 1989; 196 member countries — the largest international police organization
Not a police force itself — has no agents who make arrests; facilitates cooperation between national police forces
Red Notices are requests to locate and provisionally arrest a person pending extradition — often called “international arrest warrants” (though technically they are not)
Issues color-coded notices: Red (arrest), Blue (locate), Yellow (missing persons), Black (unidentified bodies), and others
Full name: International Criminal Police Organization (ICPO-INTERPOL)
During WWII, the organization was effectively controlled by Nazi Germany (1938–1945)

items.prompt: Which UN agency, originally created in 1946 for post-war child relief in Europe, is known in the U.S. for its Halloween trick-or-treat donation boxes?
alternates: United Nations International Children's Emergency Fund
items.explanation:
Founded December 11, 1946 as the United Nations International Children’s Emergency Fund; name shortened but acronym kept
Headquartered in New York City; operates in over 190 countries
Originally created to provide emergency food and healthcare to children in post-WWII Europe and China
The orange trick-or-treat boxes have been a Halloween tradition since 1950 — one of the most recognized fundraising campaigns in America
Won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1965 for its work promoting children’s welfare worldwide
Goodwill Ambassadors have included Audrey Hepburn, Danny Kaye, David Beckham, and Shakira
Provides vaccines, nutrition, clean water, education, and emergency relief to children globally

items.prompt: Which Paris-based UN agency is responsible for designating World Heritage Sites and promoting international cooperation in education, science, and culture?
alternates: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization
items.explanation:
Founded November 16, 1945; constitution came into force in 1946
Headquartered in Paris, France — one of the few major UN agencies not based in New York or Geneva
Designates World Heritage Sites: over 1,200 cultural and natural sites in 170 countries as of 2025
Also maintains lists of Intangible Cultural Heritage (traditions, performing arts, crafts)
The U.S. withdrew in 1984 (rejoined 2003), withdrew again in 2018 under Trump, and rejoined in 2023
Israel also withdrew in 2018 after UNESCO recognized Palestinian membership
Promotes literacy, press freedom (World Press Freedom Day, May 3), and scientific cooperation worldwide

items.prompt: Which London-based human rights organization, founded in 1961 after a British lawyer read about two Portuguese students imprisoned for raising a toast to freedom, won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1977?
items.explanation:
Founded in London in July 1961 by British lawyer Peter Benenson
Headquartered in London, United Kingdom; over 10 million supporters worldwide
Began with Benenson’s article “The Forgotten Prisoners” in The Observer newspaper (May 28, 1961)
Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1977 for its work defending human rights and prisoners of conscience
Campaigns against torture, the death penalty, political imprisonment, and extrajudicial killings
The candle wrapped in barbed wire is its iconic logo — symbolizing hope amid oppression
Coined the term “prisoner of conscience” for people jailed solely for their beliefs, ethnicity, or identity
